+{"id":"gt-01u","title":"Design: Collapse mail into beads","description":"## Proposal

Replace the separate mail system (JSONL inboxes) with beads issues using a naming convention.

## Rationale

If all state should be in beads (work items, swarm state, dependencies), why have a separate system for messages? Mail is just:
- Handoffs (agent → self)
- Commands (Mayor → Refinery)
- Escalations (Witness → Mayor)

These can all be beads issues with a special prefix.

## Design

### Convention
- Messages use `@-` prefix: `@-witness-1734012345`
- Assignee = recipient
- Status: open = unread, closed = read/acknowledged
- Priority 0 = urgent

### Commands (thin wrappers)
```bash
gt mail send witness -s "Subject" -m "Body"
  → bd create --prefix=@ --title="Subject" --assignee=witness --description="Body"

gt mail inbox
  → bd list --prefix=@ --assignee=$(gt whoami) --status=open

gt mail read @-abc
  → bd show @-abc && bd close @-abc
```

### Notification
Daemon watches for new `@-` issues and pokes relevant sessions.
Or: agents poll on heartbeat (simpler).

## What We Remove
- `mail/inbox.jsonl` files
- Mail JSONL read/write code
- Separate delivery mechanism

## What We Keep
- `gt mail` CLI (as wrapper)
- Handoff semantics
- Notification (via daemon or polling)

## Benefits
- One system, one sync, one query interface
- All communication in git history
- Simpler architecture

## Risks
- Beads prefix filtering must be efficient
- Namespace collision with user prefixes
- Performance for high-frequency messages (probably fine for handoffs)

## Decision Point
Do we need first-class mail support in beads (`bd mail` commands) or is convention sufficient?","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-16T02:10:18.32879-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-16T02:10:18.32879-08:00"}

 {"id":"gt-0pc","title":"Document Overseer role (human operator)","description":"Document the Overseer role in Gas Town architecture.

## The Overseer

The **Overseer** is the human operator of Gas Town. Not an agent - a person.

## Responsibilities

| Area | Overseer Does | Mayor/Agents Do |
|------|---------------|-----------------|
| Strategy | Define project goals | Execute toward goals |
| Priorities | Set priority order | Work in priority order |
| Escalations | Final decision on stuck work | Escalate to Overseer |
| Resources | Provision machines | Use allocated resources |
| Quality | Review & approve swarm output | Produce output |
| Operations | Run gt commands, monitor dashboards | Do the work |

## Key Interactions

### Overseer → Mayor
- Start/stop Mayor sessions
- Direct Mayor via conversation
- Review Mayor recommendations
- Approve cross-rig decisions

### Mayor → Overseer (Escalations)
- Stuck workers after retries
- Resource decisions (add machines, polecats)
- Ambiguous requirements
- Architecture decisions

## Operating Cadence

Typical Overseer workflow:
1. Morning: Check status, review overnight work
2. During day: Monitor, respond to escalations, adjust priorities
3. End of day: Review progress, plan next batch

## Commands for Overseers

```bash
gt status              # Quick health check
gt doctor              # Detailed diagnostics  
gt doctor --fix        # Auto-repair issues
gt inbox               # Messages from agents
gt stop --all          # Emergency halt
```

## Documentation Updates

- Overseer section under Agent Roles
- Clarify Mayor reports to Overseer
- Add Overseer to workflow diagrams","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-15T23:18:03.177633-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T23:22:51.477786-08:00","closed_at":"2025-12-15T23:22:51.477786-08:00","close_reason":"Overseer role documented in docs/architecture.md"}
 {"id":"gt-17r","title":"Doctor check: Zombie session cleanup","description":"Detect and clean up zombie tmux sessions via gt doctor.

## Problem

Zombie sessions occur when:
- Agent crashes without cleanup
- gt kill fails mid-operation
- System restart leaves orphan sessions
- Session naming collision

## Checks

### ZombieSessionCheck
- List all tmux sessions matching gt-* pattern
- Cross-reference with known polecats
- Flag sessions with no corresponding polecat state
- Flag sessions for removed polecats
- Check session age vs polecat creation time

### Detection Criteria
- Session exists but polecat directory doesn't
- Session name doesn't match any registered polecat
- Polecat state=idle but session running
- Multiple sessions for same polecat

## Output

```
[WARN] Zombie tmux sessions detected:
  - gt-wyvern-OldPolecat (polecat removed)
  - gt-beads-Unknown (no matching polecat)
  - gt-wyvern-Toast (duplicate session)

  Run 'gt doctor --fix' to clean up
```

## Auto-Fix (--fix flag)

- Kill orphan tmux sessions
- Update polecat state to match reality
- Log all cleanup actions

## Safety

- Never kill sessions where polecat state=working
- Prompt before killing if --fix used without --force
- Create audit log of killed sessions","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-15T23:18:01.446702-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T23:18:39.517644-08:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"gt-17r","depends_on_id":"gt-f9x.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-15T23:19:05.66301-08:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"gt-1le","title":"town handoff command (optional)","description":"CLI commands for session handoff workflow (optional convenience).

## Commands

### gt handoff
Generate handoff interactively.
```
gt handoff [--send]
```
- Collects current state (status, inbox, beads)
- Prompts for additional notes
- --send: Mail to self and exit

### gt resume
Check for and display pending handoff.
```
gt resume
```
- Checks inbox for handoff message
- Displays formatted handoff if found
- Suggests next actions

## Implementation

These are convenience wrappers. The same workflow can be done manually:
```bash
# Manual handoff
town status > /tmp/handoff
town inbox >> /tmp/handoff
bd ready >> /tmp/handoff
# Edit and send
town mail send mayor/ -s "Session Handoff" -f /tmp/handoff
```

## Priority

P2 - Optional. Manual workflow works fine. Nice to have for UX.

## Checks

### SwarmHealthCheck
- List all active swarms (polecats with state=working)
- Check last activity timestamp for each
- Flag as potentially stuck if no progress for configurable threshold (default: 30 min)
- Check if Witness is running for the rig
- Verify Witness last heartbeat time

### Stuck Detection Criteria
- Polecat state=working but session not running
- Polecat state=working but output unchanged for threshold
- Witness not responding to health checks
- Multiple polecats in same rig all stuck

  Suggestions:
  - gt witness status wyvern
  - gt capture wyvern/Toast 50
  - gt stop --rig wyvern (kill all)
```

## Beads Access Section for Prompting

```markdown
## Beads Access

You have **full beads access** - create, update, and close issues.

### Quick Reference

```bash
# View work
bd ready                    # Issues ready (no blockers)
bd list                     # All open issues
bd show <id>                # Issue details

# Create issues
bd create --title="Fix bug" --type=bug --priority=2
bd create --title="Add feature" --type=feature

# Update issues
bd update <id> --status=in_progress    # Claim work
bd close <id>                          # Mark complete

# Sync (required before merge!)
bd sync                     # Commit beads changes to git
```

### When to Create Issues

Create beads issues when you discover work that:
- Is outside your current task scope
- Would benefit from tracking
- Should be done by someone else

**Good examples**:
```bash
bd create --title="Race condition in auth" --type=bug --priority=1
bd create --title="Document API rate limits" --type=task --priority=3
```

**Don't create for**:
- Tiny fixes you can do in 2 minutes
- Vague "improvements" with no scope
- Work already tracked elsewhere

### Beads Sync Protocol

**CRITICAL**: Always sync beads before merging!

```bash
bd sync                    # Commits beads changes
git add .beads/
git commit -m "beads: sync"
```

If you forget to sync, beads changes are lost when session ends.
```

 {"id":"gt-eu9","title":"Witness session cycling and handoff","description":"Add session cycling and handoff protocol to Witness CLAUDE.md template.

## Session Cycling Protocol

```markdown
## Session Cycling

Your context will fill over long swarms. Proactively cycle when:
- Running for many hours
- Losing track of which workers you've checked
- Responses getting slower
- About to start complex operation

### Handoff Protocol

1. **Capture current state**:
```bash
town list .           # Worker states
town all beads        # Pending verifications  
town inbox            # Unprocessed messages
```

2. **Compose handoff note**:
```
[HANDOFF_TYPE]: witness_cycle
[TIMESTAMP]: <now>
[RIG]: <rig>

## Active Workers
<list workers and status>

## Pending Verifications
<workers signaled done but not verified>

## Recent Actions
<last 3-5 actions>

## Warnings/Notes
<anything next session should know>

## Next Steps
<what should happen next>
```

3. **Send handoff**:
```bash
town mail send <rig>/witness -s "Session Handoff" -m "<note>"
```

4. **Exit cleanly**: End session, daemon spawns fresh one.

### On Fresh Session Start

1. Check for handoff: `town inbox | grep "Session Handoff"`
2. If found, read it and resume from handoff state
3. If not found, do full status check
```

## Implementation

 {"id":"gt-gl2","title":"Clarify Mayor vs Witness cleanup responsibilities","description":"Document the cleanup authority model: Witness owns ALL per-worker cleanup, Mayor never involved.

## The Rule

**Witness handles ALL per-worker cleanup. Mayor is never involved.**

## Why This Matters

1. Separation of concerns: Mayor strategic, Witness operational
2. Reduced coordination overhead: No back-and-forth for routine cleanup
3. Faster shutdown: Witness kills workers immediately upon verification
4. Cleaner escalation: Mayor only hears about problems

## What Witness Handles

- Verifying worker git state before kill
- Nudging workers to fix dirty state
- Killing worker sessions
- Updating worker state (sleep/wake)
- Logging verification results

## What Mayor Handles

- Receiving swarm complete notifications
- Deciding whether to start new swarms
- Handling escalations (stuck workers after 3 retries)
- Cross-rig coordination

## Escalation Path

Worker stuck -> Witness nudges (up to 3x) -> Witness escalates to Mayor -> Mayor decides: force kill, reassign, or human

## Anti-Patterns

DO NOT: Mayor asks Witness if worker X is clean
DO: Witness reports swarm complete, all workers verified

DO NOT: Mayor kills worker sessions directly
DO: Mayor tells Witness to abort swarm, Witness handles cleanup

DO NOT: Workers report done to Mayor
DO: Workers report to Witness, Witness aggregates and reports up","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-15T19:48:56.678724-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T20:48:12.068964-08:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"gt-gl2","depends_on_id":"gt-82y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-15T19:49:05.929877-08:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"gt-iib","title":"Architecture: Decentralized rig structure with per-rig agents","description":"## Decision

Adopt decentralized architecture where each rig contains all its agents (mayor/, witness/, refinery/, polecats/) rather than centralizing mayor clones at town level.

## Town Level Structure

```
~/ai/                              # Town root
├── config/                        # Town config (VISIBLE, not hidden)
│   ├── town.json                  # {"type": "town"}
│   ├── rigs.json                  # Registry of managed rigs
│   └── federation.json            # Wasteland config (future)
│
├── mayor/                         # Mayor's HOME at town level
│   ├── CLAUDE.md
│   ├── mail/inbox.jsonl
│   └── state.json
│
└── <rigs>/                        # Managed projects
```

## Rig Level Structure (e.g., wyvern)

```
wyvern/                            # Rig = clone of project repo

```

## Key Decisions

1. **Visible config dir**: `config/` not `.gastown/` (models don't find hidden dirs)
2. **Witness per-rig**: Each rig has its own Witness (pit boss) with its own clone
3. **Mayor decentralized**: Mayor's clones live IN each rig at `<rig>/mayor/rig/`
4. **Minimal invasiveness**: Only `.git/info/exclude` modified, no commits to project
5. **Clone subdir name**: Keep `rig/` for consistency (refinery/rig/, witness/rig/, mayor/rig/)

## Role Detection

- Rig root → Mayor (canonical main)

- <rig>/refinery/rig/ → Refinery
- <rig>/witness/rig/ → Witness
- <rig>/polecats/<name>/ → Polecat

## Heartbeat Actions

Every N seconds (configurable):

1. Check Witness health per rig
   - Poke if witness needs to nudge polecats
   
2. Check Refinery queue per rig
   - Poke if pending work in queue
   
3. Check swarm completion
   - If all tasks merged but not landed, poke Refinery

## Eventually Convergent

Multiple signals reinforce state:
- Polecat signals done → Witness notices → pokes Refinery
- Daemon heartbeat → checks queue → pokes Refinery
- Beads status → queryable by any agent

Even if one signal missed, system converges.

## Interface

```go
func (d *Daemon) HeartbeatLoop() {
    for {
        for _, rig := range d.rigs {
            d.CheckWitness(rig)
            d.CheckRefinery(rig)
            d.CheckSwarms(rig)
        }
        time.Sleep(d.config.HeartbeatInterval)
    }
}
```

## Notifications

Use mail with low priority for heartbeat pokes.

 {"id":"gt-kmn.4","title":"Refinery semantic merge processing loop","description":"Implement the Refinery semantic merge loop for processing polecat work.

## Overview

The Refinery is a fully empowered Claude Code agent that:
1. Fetches polecat branches
2. Merges to integration branch
3. Resolves conflicts semantically (re-implements if needed)
4. Runs tests
5. Escalates only when truly stuck

## Processing Loop

1. Check queue for completed tasks (polecat done, not yet merged)
2. For each task:
   - Fetch polecat branch
   - Attempt merge to integration/<swarm-id>
   - On conflict: semantic resolution (understand intent, re-implement)
   - Run tests if configured
   - On success: mark merged, file bead, notify witness
   - On failure: notify polecat (test fail) or escalate (stuck)
3. When all tasks merged: land integration to main

## Semantic Merge

When git merge fails, Refinery:
1. Reads polecat changes and beads issue
2. Understands intent
3. Re-implements on current integration branch
4. Only escalates if truly blocked

## Daemon Poking

Refinery is poked by:
- Daemon heartbeat (periodic)
- Witness notification (polecat done)
- Mayor request (check queue)

## Dependencies

Needs: gt-u1j.6 (mail), gt-u1j.3 (git), gt-u1j.14 (merge queue)

## Processing Loop

1. Check queue for pending MRs

   b. Attempt merge to refinery/rig (local main)

   d. If pass: push to origin, mark merged
   e. If fail: mark rejected, notify polecat
3. Sleep, repeat

## Merge Strategy

- Fast-forward when possible
- Merge commit when not
- On conflict: reject MR, polecat must rebase

If tests configured:
```bash
cd refinery/rig
git merge polecat/branch
<test_command>  # e.g., go test ./...
```
Result determines merge/reject.

## Notifications

On merge success:
- Mail to polecat: "Your work merged"
- Update bead if issue tracked

On merge failure:
- Mail to polecat: "Merge failed: <reason>"
- Include conflict details if applicable

Current CLI agent session cycling is painful:
- Shell → CC starts → priming → context loads → ready → work → exit/crash → repeat
- Each cycle is 30-60 seconds of cold boot
- No continuity between shell and agent's inner state
- Raw "session not running, starting..." loop is the baseline

## GGT Advantages (already have)

- Beads: Work state survives session death completely
- Mail: Handoff notes from past-self to future-self  
- Prime commands: Structured context reload

## Gap: Transition Mechanics

Ideas to explore when actively using CLI:

1. **In-band cycling** - `/restart` or `/cycle` command, agent handles own restart without dropping to shell

2. **Hot standby** - TUI maintains pre-warmed session in background, switch to already-primed agent

3. **Persistent wrapper** - Bubbletea TUI stays running across session cycles, CC sessions come/go inside it

4. **Session pooling** - Keep 2-3 primed sessions ready, never wait for cold start

## Deferred

 {"id":"gt-v5k","title":"Design: Failure modes and recovery","description":"Document failure modes and recovery strategies for Gas Town operations.

## Critical Failure Modes

### 1. Agent Crash Mid-Operation

**Scenario**: Polecat crashes while committing, Witness crashes while verifying

**Detection**:
- Session suddenly gone (tmux check fails)
- State shows 'working' but no session
- Heartbeat stops (for Witness)

**Recovery**:
- Doctor detects via ZombieSessionCheck
- Capture any recoverable state
- Reset agent state to 'idle'
- For Witness: auto-restart via supervisor or manual gt witness start

### 2. Git State Corruption

**Scenario**: Merge conflict, failed rebase, detached HEAD

**Detection**:
- Git commands fail
- Dirty state that won't commit
- Branch diverged from origin

**Recovery**:
- gt doctor reports git health issues
- Manual intervention recommended
- Severe cases: remove clone, re-clone

### 3. Beads Sync Conflict

**Scenario**: Two polecats modify same issue

**Detection**:
- bd sync fails with conflict
- Beads tombstone mechanism handles most cases

**Recovery**:
- Beads has last-write-wins semantics
- bd sync --force in extreme cases
- Issues may need manual dedup

### 4. Tmux Failure

**Scenario**: Tmux server crashes, socket issues

**Detection**:
- All sessions inaccessible
- "no server running" errors

**Recovery**:
- Kill any orphan processes
- tmux kill-server && tmux start-server
- All agent states reset to idle
- Re-spawn active work

### 5. Claude API Issues

**Scenario**: Rate limits, outages, context limits

**Detection**:
- Sessions hang or produce errors
- Repeated failure patterns

**Recovery**:
- Exponential backoff (handled by Claude Code)
- For context limits: session cycling (mail-to-self)
- For outages: wait and retry

### 6. Disk Full

**Scenario**: Clones, logs, or beads fill disk

**Detection**:
- Write operations fail
- git/bd commands error

**Recovery**:
- Clean up logs: rm ~/.gastown/logs/*
- Remove old polecat clones
- gt doctor --fix can clean some cruft

### 7. Network Failure

**Scenario**: Can't reach GitHub, API servers

**Detection**:
- git fetch/push fails
- Claude sessions hang

**Recovery**:
- Work continues locally
- Queue pushes for later
- Sync when connectivity restored

## Recovery Principles

1. **Fail safe**: Prefer stopping over corrupting
2. **State is recoverable**: Git and beads have recovery mechanisms
3. **Doctor heals**: gt doctor --fix handles common issues
4. **Emergency stop**: gt stop --all as last resort

 {"id":"gt-zx3","title":"Per-rig beads repo configuration","description":"Add per-rig beads configuration to rig config schema.

## Config Schema

In each rig's config.json:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "name": "wyvern",
  "git_url": "https://github.com/steveyegge/wyvern",
  "beads": {
    "repo": "local",    // "local" | "<path>" | "<git-url>"
    "root": null,       // Override bd --root (optional)
    "prefix": "wyv"     // Issue prefix for this rig
  }
}
```

## Repo Options

| Value | Meaning | Use Case |
|-------|---------|----------|
| `"local"` | Use project's `.beads/` | Own projects, full commit access |
| `"<path>"` | Use beads at path | OSS contributions |
| `"<git-url>"` | Clone and use repo | Team shared beads |

## Environment Injection

When spawning polecats, Gas Town sets:
```bash
export BEADS_ROOT="<resolved-path>"
```

## Resolution Logic

```go
func ResolveBeadsRoot(rigConfig *RigConfig, rigPath string) (string, error) {
    beads := rigConfig.Beads
    switch {
    case beads.Root != "":
        return beads.Root, nil
    case beads.Repo == "local" || beads.Repo == "":
        return filepath.Join(rigPath, ".beads"), nil
    case strings.HasPrefix(beads.Repo, "/"):
        return beads.Repo, nil
    case strings.Contains(beads.Repo, "://"):
        return cloneAndResolve(beads.Repo)
    default:
        return filepath.Join(rigPath, beads.Repo), nil
    }
}
```

## Backwards Compatibility

If `beads` section missing, assume `"repo": "local"`.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-15T19:47:16.660049-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T20:48:02.122203-08:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"gt-zx3","depends_on_id":"gt-l3c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-15T19:47:35.726502-08:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
